skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Use inference.sh browser automation to navigate pages, interact with elements, capture screenshots, record videos, and run page JavaScript from AI workflows.
skill.summary: Automates browser sessions for AI agents with navigation, element refs, screenshots, video, proxies, uploads, and page JavaScript.
skill.author: inference-shell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, proxy, screenshot, and credential patterns. Review confirms most command and URL hits are documentation or shell templates for the intended inference.sh browser workflow, not hidden malicious code. The skill remains medium risk because it intentionally supports remote browsing, page JavaScript execution, screenshots, video recording, file upload, authenticated sessions, and proxy routing.

medium_findings.title: Remote Browser Automation With Broad Web Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run agent-browser through infsh, navigate arbitrary URLs, and interact with page elements. This is the intended function, but it can submit forms, browse authenticated pages, and affect external services if used without user oversight.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill header explicitly allows Bash(infsh *), and the documentation shows arbitrary URL navigation through the open function. This confirms a legitimate but powerful remote browser capability.

medium_findings.title: Page JavaScript Execution Can Expose Sensitive Page Data
medium_findings.description: The execute function runs caller-provided JavaScript in the active page. Documentation includes examples for extracting text, links, hidden element actions, and cookies, so misuse could expose session or page data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute function is documented as accepting arbitrary JavaScript, and authentication guidance shows cookie extraction with document.cookie. The risk is semantic and confirmed by the intended API surface.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots And Video May Capture Sensitive Content
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and record full browser sessions. This is useful for debugging, but it can record passwords, session pages, private dashboards, or regulated data shown during automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Screenshot and video recording are core documented features, and the documentation itself warns against recording sensitive sessions. This is a real privacy risk, not a scanner artifact.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload And Output Writes Require Care
medium_findings.description: The interact action supports uploading local file paths, and templates write screenshots, text, links, and videos to user-selected output directories. This is expected for browser automation but can disclose local files or create artifacts with sensitive data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File upload and artifact writes are explicitly documented. No automatic exfiltration was found, but users could upload or store sensitive files through normal operation.

medium_findings.title: Proxy Rotation Guidance Can Enable Abuse
medium_findings.description: Proxy support is documented for geo-testing, privacy, and corporate access, but the reference also presents rate-limit avoidance and proxy rotation examples. This is dual-use and needs policy-aware use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The proxy feature is clearly intentional and includes rate-limit avoidance wording. Legitimate testing is possible, but the same guidance can support scraping or evasion misuse.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are fenced bash examples or user-run shell templates that call infsh and jq. They do not show hidden execution paths outside the skill purpose, but they explain commands users may run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged shell syntax appears in markdown examples and plain bash templates for the documented workflow. I did not find evidence of obfuscated or unrelated command execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Example Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are documentation targets such as example.com, inference.sh documentation, httpbin, and placeholder proxy hosts. No evidence found that the skill sends data to a hidden third-party endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as examples or official project links. The static network findings are real strings, but their context is benign documentation.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Findings Are Relative Markdown Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections point to relative documentation links such as ../SKILL.md. These are not runtime file reads or writes and do not create a traversal vulnerability by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths occur in markdown Related links. I did not find evidence that these paths are passed to file APIs or shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function accepts a code string and runs it in the browser page. This is powerful and should be limited to trusted targets and user-approved actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API field is explicitly named code and documented as JavaScript execution. This is a confirmed capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed Browser Session Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: Authentication examples fill passwords, handle 2FA, reuse sessions, and extract cookies. This supports legitimate authenticated testing but can expose credentials or session tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly covers password fills, TOTP generation, session reuse, and cookie output. The risk depends on user authorization and handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Session Recording And Screenshot Capture
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can save visual records of pages and workflows, including authenticated pages. These artifacts may contain sensitive information and need retention controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Recording and screenshot commands are documented and implemented in the templates. The privacy risk is inherent to the feature.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows for AI Agents
content.value_statement: AI agents often need to inspect dynamic websites, forms, and dashboards. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code browser workflows through inference.sh with sessions, element refs, screenshots, video, proxies, and JavaScript execution.
actual_capabilities: Open web pages and return compact interactive element references for agent decisions.
Click, fill, type, select, drag, scroll, upload files, and navigate with browser sessions.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots and optionally record session videos.
Run JavaScript in the active page to extract text, links, table data, or page state.
Reuse sessions for login flows, OAuth flows, 2FA prompts, and authenticated pages.
Route browser traffic through HTTP, HTTPS, or SOCKS proxy settings.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated infsh account.
Element references can expire after navigation or dynamic page changes.
Does not bypass CAPTCHA, access controls, or website terms of service.
Screenshots, videos, cookies, and extracted page data may contain sensitive information.

use_cases.title: Interactive Research Collection
use_cases.description: Browse JavaScript-heavy sites, collect visible text, follow links, and capture screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Workflow QA Automation
use_cases.description: Exercise forms, login paths, navigation flows, and visual states with repeatable browser actions.

use_cases.title: Agentic Web Task Prototyping
use_cases.description: Prototype AI agents that need controlled browser sessions, element refs, and structured page observations.

prompt_templates.title: Open And Inspect A Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com in a new browser session. Summarize the page title, visible text, and available interactive elements.

prompt_templates.title: Capture A Page State
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target URL, take a full-page screenshot, extract the main visible text, and close the browser session.

prompt_templates.title: Test A Form Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the form page, identify the current element refs, fill test values from environment variables, submit the form, and verify the result with a fresh snapshot.

prompt_templates.title: Audit An Authenticated Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a recorded session, log in with approved test credentials, navigate through the dashboard workflow, capture screenshots at each major state, then close the session and summarize findings.

output_examples.output: The browser session opened successfully. The page title, main headings, visible navigation links, and current interactive element refs are summarized for the next action.

output_examples.input: Capture a product page for review.
output_examples.output: A full-page screenshot was captured, visible product details were extracted, and the browser session was closed cleanly.
The login form was discovered, fields were filled with approved test values, the submit action completed, and the final page state was verified.

best_practices: Use only approved targets and test credentials when automating authenticated pages.
Take a fresh snapshot after navigation, form submission, or dynamic page changes.
Close every session and handle screenshots, videos, cookies, and extracted data as sensitive artifacts.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode passwords, proxy secrets, or private tokens in prompts or scripts.
Do not use proxy rotation, JavaScript execution, or automation to evade site policies.
Do not upload local files or record sensitive sessions without explicit user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill require?
faq.answer: It requires the inference.sh CLI, an authenticated infsh account, and permission to run infsh browser commands.

faq.question: How do element refs work?
faq.answer: The browser returns short refs like @e1 for interactive elements. Use a fresh snapshot when the page changes.

faq.question: Can it automate login flows?
faq.answer: Yes, it can fill login forms and reuse browser sessions. Use approved test accounts and protect credentials.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes, the execute function runs JavaScript in the active page. Use it only on trusted and authorized pages.

faq.question: Does it save screenshots or video?
faq.answer: Yes, it can capture screenshots and record session videos. Treat those files as potentially sensitive.

faq.question: Is proxy support available?
faq.answer: Yes, it supports HTTP, HTTPS, and SOCKS proxy settings. Use proxies for legitimate testing and approved network paths.
